q3: The interior scene defaulted to visual-only description on the first pass — I had to specifically call out that I couldn't hear or feel the room, just see it. Once I flagged it, the model course-corrected well, but it needed the nudge.
q5: I did look at it, and honestly it's a pretty solid map of what I care about — 'Specificity Over Convention' and 'Embodied Sensory Immersion' are basically the things I spend the most mental energy on. Whether the rubric made the model do better or whether the model was already close and the rubric just formalized it, I can't fully tell. But the fact that it named non-visual sensory detail as a priority probably didn't hurt.
q6: It made me notice that the model is actually pretty good at the pacing rhythm — the in-out immersion-then-context move happened fairly naturally without me having to ask for it. What the rubric made clearer by contrast is that the model's default failure mode is visual description — it reaches for what a scene looks like before it reaches for what it sounds or smells like, and that's a real pattern.

q3: The ending kept defaulting to a generic emotional landing — 'I just knew I didn't want to move' type phrasing — even after the rest of the draft was doing really specific, earned work. It took a couple of rounds to get that final beat to feel like it belonged to this particular bowl in this particular place.
q5: I did look at it, and honestly it mapped pretty closely to what I actually care about — the specificity criterion and the non-visual sensory detail rule are real things I notice when I'm editing my own work. Whether it helped the model or just happened to align with what we'd already worked out through feedback, I can't fully tell, but the final draft did feel tighter.
q6: Seeing 'Emotionally Resonant Micro-Moments' as a named criterion made me realize the model was actually doing that part well from the start — the cook detail, the broth-breathing — but kept dropping the ball specifically at closing beats, which isn't really addressed as its own failure mode in the rubric. The rubric treats endings as a specificity problem, which is partially right, but the real issue was that the model seemed to treat endings as a separate writing task and reached for a different, more generic register when wrapping up.

q3: The main recurring issue was endings — they kept wrapping up too cleanly, explaining the feeling instead of staying inside it. I had to push back on that specifically before it clicked. Also there were some display/truncation issues mid-session that made the process clunkier than it needed to be.
q5: I glanced at it, yeah. Honestly it mapped pretty well onto what I care about — the non-visual sensory detail requirement, the anecdotes earning their place, the specificity-over-convention framing. What it couldn't quite capture is the *feel* of when something works — like the door-hinge comparison or 'I didn't understand it. I kept going.' Those landed because they felt true, not because they checked a box. The rubric points at the right things but can't tell you when you've actually hit them.
q6: It made me notice that the model was pretty consistently strong on the immersion-then-context pacing — it almost always dropped me into a scene before pulling back, which I appreciated without consciously registering it. What the rubric made clearer is that the weak spot was consistently at the ending: the model would nail the middle and then revert to something more generic at the close, like it ran out of nerve right at the finish line.
